elements.sent-2411-001: The offline reinforcement learning (RL) problem aims to learn an optimal policy from historical data collected by one or more behavioural policies (experts) by interacting with an environment.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the individual experts may be privacy-sensitive in that the learnt policy may retain information about their precise choices.
elements.sent-2411-003: In some domains like personalized retrieval, advertising and healthcare, the expert choices are considered sensitive data.
elements.sent-2411-004: To provably protect the privacy of such experts, we propose a novel consensus-based expert-level differentially private offline RL training approach compatible with any existing offline RL algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-005: We prove rigorous differential privacy guarantees, while maintaining strong empirical performance.
elements.sent-2411-006: Unlike existing work in differentially private RL, we supplement the theory with proof-of-concept experiments on classic RL environments featuring large continuous state spaces, demonstrating substantial improvements over a natural baseline across multiple tasks.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.92\linewidth]{imgs/algorithm2.jpg} \caption{Training pipeline for Expert-level Differentially Private Offline RL given an expert set $\Pi = \{\pi_1, \pi_2, \dots, \pi_m\}$ and input trajectories logged into an offline dataset $D_\Pi$. $\mathcal{A}_{DR}$ (Algorithm \ref{alg:data_release}) splits input trajectories and adds stable prefixes to $D^\Pi_{stable}$ discarding the rest to $D^\Pi_{unst}$. These are used to train any off-the-shelf offline RL algorithm as described in Algorithm \ref{alg:selective_dpsgd} and learn an expert-level private policy $\pi_{private}$.} \label{fig:my_label}
elements.sent-2411-007: Leveraging existing offline datasets to learn high-quality decision policies via offline Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a critical requirement in many settings ranging from robotics~\citep{fu2020d4rl, levine2020offline and recommendation systems~\citep{bottou2013counterfactual, ie2019reinforcement, cai2017real to healthcare applications~\citep{oberst2019counterfactual, tang2022leveraging .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Correspondingly, there is now a rich literature on effective techniques~\citep{fujimoto2019off, kumar2019stabilizing, kumar2020conservative, cheng2022adversarially for learning from such offline datasets, collected using one or more behaviour policies.}
elements.sent-2411-009: An often overlooked aspect of this literature is, however, that in privacy sensitive domains such as personalized retrieval, advertising, and healthcare, the behaviour policies might reveal private information about the preferences / strategies used by the corresponding experts (users, advertisers, health care providers) whose decisions underlie the offline data.
elements.sent-2411-010: In such scenarios, we ideally seek to uncover the broadly beneficial strategies employed in making decisions by a large cohort of experts whose demonstrations the offline data is collected with, while not leaking the private information of any given expert.
elements.sent-2411-011: We call this novel setting offline RL with expert-level privacy, and design algorithms with strong privacy guarantees for it.
elements.sent-2411-012: There has been a growing interest in differentially private RL, both in online~\citep{wang2019privacy, qiao2023near,zhou2022dprl,liao2023local and offline~\citep{qiao2024offline settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The offline RL literature, which is most relevant to this work, primarily focuses on protecting privacy at the trajectory-level.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, protecting the privacy of an expert, who might contribute multiple trajectories to the demonstration dataset, requires significantly more careful techniques.
elements.sent-2411-015: Secondly, the prior works mostly focus on tabular or linear settings~\citep{qiao2023near,qiao2024offline,zhou2022dprl , where the offline RL problem can be effectively solved using count or linear regression based techniques, and is hence amenable to existing techniques for privatizing counts or linear regression models.}
elements.sent-2411-016: In contrast, we make no assumptions on the size of the state space, the parameterization of the learned policy or the policy underlying expert demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-017: We identify and formalize the question of offline RL with expert-level privacy and motivate it with practical examples.
elements.sent-2411-018: We also explain the inadequacy of prior algorithms in this setting.
elements.sent-2411-019: We provide practical algorithms for offline RL with strong expert-level privacy guarantees, building on top of any gradient-based offline RL algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-020: In contrast with prior approaches which use noisy statistics of the data in offline RL~\citep{qiao2024offline , we develop a novel scheme which also identifies a subset of the data which can be used in learning without any added noise.}
elements.sent-2411-021: This capability, which we describe in more detail below, is not feasible in the typical trajectory-level privacy settings, and highlights the benefits of systematically studying the expert-level privacy setting.
elements.sent-2411-022: We perform a proof-of-concept evaluation of our algorithm on standard RL benchmarks and compare our approach with an adaptation of DP-SGD to expert-level privacy as a baseline for privatizing the gradient-based offline RL algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-023: We show that our approach significantly improves upon this baseline across multiple test environments.

elements.sent-2411-044: We start by introducing the relevant background in offline RL and differential privacy, before formally defining the setting of offline RL with expert-level privacy.
elements.sent-2411-045: Offline RL is a data-driven approach to RL that aims to circumvent the prohibitive cost of interactive data collection in many real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-046: Under this paradigm, a static dataset collected by some behaviour policy $\pi_\beta$ is used to train another policy $\pi$ without any further interaction with the environment.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{PrefixQuery ($\mathcal{A}_{PQ}$): Tests if count of experts expected to execute a trajectory is large enough} \label{alg:prefix_query} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Expert policies $\Pi$, trajectory $\tau = (s_1, a_1, s_2, a_2, \dots)$, stability threshold $\hat{\theta}$, $\epsilon$ \State Compute query count as \State $count_{\tau}(\Pi) \gets \sum_{\pi \in \Pi} \prod_{j=1}^{|\tau|} \pi(a_j | s_j)$ \If{$count_{\tau}(\Pi) + Lap(\frac{4}{\epsilon}) > \hat{\theta}$} \State Return $\top$ \Else \State Return $\perp$ \EndIf \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{DataRelease ($\mathcal{A}_{DR})$: Releasing public dataset after privatisation} \label{alg:data_release} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Expert dataset $D^\Pi$, expert policies $\Pi$, $\epsilon_1$, $\delta_1$, min action-selection probability $p_{min}$, unstable query cutoff $T \leq N \cdot m$, trajectory length $L$ \State $\epsilon' \gets \frac{\epsilon_1}{ \sqrt{32T\log(2/\delta_1)}}, \quad \delta' \gets \frac{\delta_1}{(2TL)}$ \State $c_{min} \gets \frac{e^{\epsilon'}}{(e^{\epsilon'} - 1)}, \quad \theta \gets \frac{c_{min}}{p_{min}}, \quad D^\Pi_{stable} \gets \{\}$\ \State Reshuffle the order of $D^\Pi$ \For{$c = 1, \dots, T$} \State $\tau \gets$ next trajectory from $D^\Pi$ \State $\hat{\theta} \gets \theta + \frac{4}{\epsilon'} \log(1/\delta')+ Lap(\frac{2}{\epsilon'})$ \For{$i = 1$ to $L$} \State $r \gets \mathcal{A}_{PQ}(\Pi, \tau_{1:i}, \hat{\theta}, \epsilon')$ \Comment{Algorithm \ref{alg:prefix_query}} \If{$r = \top$} \If{$i = L$} \State $D^\Pi_{stable} \gets D^\Pi_{stable} \cup \{\tau_{1:i}\}$ \EndIf \Else \If{$i \neq 1$} \State $D^\Pi_{stable} \gets D^\Pi_{stable} \cup \{\tau_{1:i-1}\}$ \EndIf \State break \EndIf \EndFor \EndFor \State return $D^\Pi_{stable}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Selective DP-SGD for Offline RL} \label{alg:selective_dpsgd} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Stable dataset $D^\Pi_{stable}$, Unstable dataset $D^\Pi_{unst}$, Sampling probability $p$, Gradient-based offline RL algorithm $\mathcal{H}_\phi$, $\epsilon_2, \delta_2$, $N$ training steps, $B$ batch size \State Initialize $\phi_0$ \For{$n=1$ to $N$} \State $b\sim Bernoulli(p)$ \If{$b=1$} \Comment{w.p. $p$} \State Sample batch of transitions $\mathcal{B} \sim D^\Pi_{unst}$ \State $\phi_{n+1} \gets$ DP-SGD($\phi_n, \mathcal{B}, \epsilon_2, \delta_2, N$) \Else \Comment{w.p. $1-p$} \State Sample batch of transitions $\mathcal{B} \sim D^\Pi_{stable}$ \State $\phi_{n+1} \gets$ SGD($\phi_n, \mathcal{B}$) \EndIf \EndFor \State return $\mathcal{H}_{\phi_N}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-141: Using all the above, we derive the following expert-level privacy guarantee for the overall training pipeline.

elements.sent-2411-144: Ensuring reasonable levels of privacy requires significant amounts of hyperparameter tuning and a large number of data points, and typically comes with a non-trivial performance impact\footnote{In supervised learning, for example, the accuracy on ImageNet training can go from $>75\%$ to $\sim47.9\%$ in the private case~\citep{kurakin2022training .}}
elements.sent-2411-145: Intuitively, with the demand for expert-level privacy in our setting, we risk severe degradation of utility since each ``record" (each expert's data) forms a much larger fraction of the overall dataset.
elements.sent-2411-146: It is thus imperative that we create a dataset which consists of diverse data collected from a large number of different experts.
elements.sent-2411-147: We describe our data generation procedure to ensure this below.
elements.sent-2411-148: We train $3000$ experts each on the Cartpole, Acrobot, LunarLander and HIV Treatment environments.
elements.sent-2411-149: Cartpole, Acrobot and LunarLander are from the Gymnasium package \citep{farama2023gym .}
elements.sent-2411-150: The HIV Treatment simulator is based on the implementation by \cite{geramifard2015rlpy of the model described in \cite{ernst2006clinical .}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Experts are trained on variations of the default environment, created by modifying the values of key parameters (e.g. gravity in LunarLander environment).
elements.sent-2411-152: The trained experts are then used to generate demonstrations on a default setting of the environment, collectively forming the aggregated dataset for offline RL.
elements.sent-2411-153: This training methodology ensures that the experts show varied (often sub-optimal) behaviours on the default environment.
elements.sent-2411-154: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:experts , the experts' return-histograms indicate that the mix of experts we create shows significant diversity in the policies learnt.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Further details regarding the environment settings used to train the expert policies are avilable in Appendix~\ref{app:expdetails .}
elements.sent-2411-156: We acknowledge that due to Assumption~\ref{ass:discrete , we do not consider continuous control domains here and focus on discrete action benchmarks.}
elements.sent-2411-157: While our algorithm allows us to use any off-the-shelf gradient-based offline RL algorithm, we use Conservative Q-Learning (CQL) \citep{kumar2020conservative algorithm for our experimental work.}
elements.sent-2411-158: More specifically, we use the DQN version of CQL, since we are dealing with discrete action spaces.
elements.sent-2411-159: To represent the Q-value function, we use a neural network with 2 hidden layers of 256 units each.
elements.sent-2411-160: During training, we perform a grid search to find the optimal set of hyper-parameters (learning rate, sampling probability $p$, DP-SGD noise) for each environment.
elements.sent-2411-161: The batch size is fixed to 256 for Acrobot, LunarLander and Cartpole, and to 64 for HIV Treatment.
elements.sent-2411-162: The maximum trajectory length is fixed to 200 for Acrobot and CartPole in the collected demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-163: While evaluating the final learned policy, the maximum episode length is again set as 200 for Acrobot, we increase this to 1000 for Cartpole for increased difficulty in an otherwise ``simple'' environment.
elements.sent-2411-164: We allow all DP-SGD training runs to progress until the privacy budget is used up completely and report statistics of episode returns averaged over $10$ evaluation runs at the end of training.
elements.sent-2411-165: We keep the privacy budget constant to $\epsilon = 10$, and $\delta = 1/m$, where $m$ is the number of experts.
elements.sent-2411-166: We compare our method against DP-SGD adapted for expert-level privacy.
elements.sent-2411-167: A brief discussion of the changes we made are presented in Section \ref{sec:algo .}
elements.sent-2411-168: A full discussion, along with analysis, is presented in the appendix for reference.
elements.sent-2411-169: We keep the underlying RL algorithm as CQL for fair comparison.
elements.sent-2411-170: Since there are no prior methods for the expert-level privacy setting, we do not have other readily available baselines.
elements.sent-2411-171: We also compare against a non-private baseline, which ignores privacy concerns and trains over the whole dataset using vanilla CQL.
elements.sent-2411-172: This may be taken as an upper-bound on the performance that we hope to achieve in the presence of privacy concerns.

elements.sent-2411-221: We modified gravity over 10 equally spaced values from 9.0 to 11.0, wind power over 10 equally spaced values between 0.0 to 5.0, and similarly turbulence over 10 equally spaced values from 0.0 to 0.5, to generate 1000 environment settings.
elements.sent-2411-222: For each of these environment settings, we trained agents using the PPO algorithm for $1e6$ updates along with hyperparameter tuning of learning rate and batch size.
elements.sent-2411-223: Each agent is a neural network with hidden-layers of sizes (512, 256, 64).
elements.sent-2411-224: For each environment setting, we chose the top 3 agents that performed the best on the corresponding environment, to form our 3000 experts.
elements.sent-2411-225: The default values of gravity, force magnitude and mass of the cart are 9.8, 10.0 and 1.0 respectively.
elements.sent-2411-226: We varied each control over 10 equally spaced values over a fixed range.
elements.sent-2411-227: For gravity, we fixed this range as (8.75, 11.0), for force magnitude as (9.0, 11.25) and for mass of the cart as (0.8, 1.25).
elements.sent-2411-228: Similar to above, for each of these 1000 environment settings, we trained agents using the PPO algorithm for $1e6$ updates while tuning the learning rate and batch size.
elements.sent-2411-229: We chose the top 3 agents from each setting to form our 3000 experts.
elements.sent-2411-230: The default values of all these parameters is 1.0.
elements.sent-2411-231: We assign the same length to both the links in each setting.
elements.sent-2411-232: We vary this length, the mass of link 1 and the mass of link 2 over 10 equally spaced values across fixed ranges for each.
elements.sent-2411-233: We fix these ranges as (0.8, 1.2) for the length of the links, (0.9, 1.1) for the mass of link 1 and (0.9, 1.1) for the mass of link 2.
elements.sent-2411-234: The combination of these gives us 1000 environment settings.
elements.sent-2411-235: Again, we trained agents using PPO for $1e6$ updates over multiple learning rates and batch sizes and picked the top 3 agents from each setting to form our 3000 experts.
elements.sent-2411-236: The actions correspond to the dosages of two drugs.
elements.sent-2411-237: The reward is a function of the health outcomes of the patient, and it also penalises the side-effects due to the quantity of drugs prescribed.
elements.sent-2411-238: Hence, the default reward function has 2 penalty terms, a penalty of 20000 on the dosage of drug 1, and a penalty of 2000 on the dosage of drug 2 (\cite{ernst2006clinical ).}
elements.sent-2411-239: In the default setting of the environment, we assume that the treatment at each steps is applied for 20 days and that there are 50 time steps in an episode.
elements.sent-2411-240: To train experts, we vary the penalty on drug 1 over 50 equally spaced values from 18750 to 21250, and the penalty on drug 2 over 20 equally spaced values from 1850 to 2150.
elements.sent-2411-241: Another variation that we introduce is whether the efficacy of the administered drug is noisy or not.
elements.sent-2411-242: So we either add no noise to the drug efficacy, or we add gaussian noise with standard deviation 0.01.
elements.sent-2411-243: All these modifications give us 2000 different environment settings.
elements.sent-2411-244: We trained agents using PPO for $2e6$ updates on each of the environment settings and over multiple learning rates and batch sizes.
elements.sent-2411-245: Each agent is represented by a neural network with a single hidden layer of size 512.
elements.sent-2411-246: We then picked the top agents from each of the 2000 environment settings to create experts.
elements.sent-2411-247: We picked 1000 more top-performing agents from the 2nd best agents of all the environment settings to get 3000 experts in total.
elements.sent-2411-248: Once we have chosen 3000 experts for any of the above environments, we modify each expert's policy so that for each state, the action with the highest probability gets $(1 - (|A|-1)\cdot p_{min )$ probability (where $|A|$ denotes the total number of actions), whereas all the other actions get $p_{min}$ probability of being executed.}
elements.sent-2411-249: We then use these modified experts to sample 20 trajectories each from the default environment to form our offline dataset.
elements.sent-2411-250: We adapted DP-SGD to the expert-level privacy setting to form the baseline that we compare against.
elements.sent-2411-251: To achieve this, during gradient updates, we ensure that an expert contributes at most one transition to a batch.
elements.sent-2411-252: To construct a batch of size $b$, we randomly sample $b$ experts from the total $m$ experts, and then pick a transition from each selected expert.
elements.sent-2411-253: This is analogous to DP-SGD training on a dataset of size $m$.
elements.sent-2411-254: For privacy accounting, we use PLD accountant from the tensorflow\_privacy library.
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Expert-Level DP-SGD (outline)} \label{alg:expert_dpsgd} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Model Parameters $\phi$, Expert Datasets $D^\Pi$, Expert Policies $\Pi$, Loss function $\mathcal{L(\phi)} = \frac{1}{b}\sum_{i=1}^b \mathcal{L}(\phi, x_i)$, Learning Rate $\eta$, Batch size $b$, Noise parameter $\sigma$, Clipping threshold $C$, Training steps $N$ \State Initialize $\phi_0$ randomly \For{$t = 1, \dots, N-1$} \State Sample subset of experts $B$ from $\Pi$ with subsampling probability $\frac{b}{m}$ \State For each $\pi_i\in B$ sample transition $x=(s, a, r, s')$ from $D^{\pi_i}$ to form a batch of transitions $B_{trans}$ \State For each $x_i\in B_{trans}$ compute gradients $g_i(\phi_t) \gets \nabla_{\phi_t} \mathcal{L}(\phi_t, x_i)$ \State Scale gradients to fit clipping threshold $\bar{g}_i(\phi_t) \gets g_i(\phi_t)/\text{max}\left( 1, \frac{\norm{g_i(\phi_t)}_2}{C}\right)$ \State Add gaussian noise to gradients $\Tilde{g}_t \gets \frac{1}{b}\left(\sum_{i=1}^b\bar{g}_i(\phi_t) + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2C^2\text{I})\right)$ \State Update model parameters $\phi_{t+1}\gets \phi_t - \eta \cdot \Tilde{g}_t$ \EndFor \State return $\phi_T$ \end{algorithmic}
